column2: HUD proposes to adopt Project Selection Criteria to set priorities for processing applications under four subsidized housing programs: Section 235(i) home mortgage interest supplements, Section 236 rental and cooperative housing interest supplements, Low-Rent Public Housing (including Leased Public Housing), and Rent Supplement. The criteria are an administrative tool to help HUD Area and insuring offices rank applications based on national needs, orderly urban development, equal opportunity, and environmental quality.
column7: All major facts, figures, and descriptions in the extracted fields are supported by the cited pages. The public_response section accurately characterizes both the commenters and HUD's responses, thou…

column2: The programs serve low- and moderate-income families, including minority households, who need decent, safe, and sanitary housing, with particular attention to metropolitan areas where integration or concentration of low-income residents is a concern. The reach is nationwide, covering hundreds of thousands of units, including roughly 325,000 Section 236 units across 50 states and 1,081 cities, 135,000 rent supplement units, 575,000 Section 235 homes, and about 929,000 public housing units.

The main alternative considered was having no formal criteria and relying on the varied judgment of Area Office staff, which past experience showed made it hard to meet program goals. Many versions of criteria were possible, ranging from more detailed standards to broader ones; the proposed version balances specificity with staff discretion and was refined through two earlier drafts published in the Federal Register.

No adverse environmental impact is expected from this administrative action, and the criteria are expected to produce generally positive environmental effects by disqualifying proposals that receive a 'poor' rating on the physical environment criterion. Criterion #5 favors projects in attractive, well-planned settings free of adverse conditions and rules out projects that would harm ecologically valuable or significant natural, historic, or archeological areas, while Criterion #4 ties approval to consistency with official land-use plans.

Comments came from a wide range of federal agencies (including HEW, AEC, Army Corps of Engineers, USDA, GSA, Interior, DOT, EPA, the Appalachian Regional Commission, and the National Capital Planning Commission) and outside groups such as the U.S. Civil Rights Commission, NAACP, League of Women Voters, National Urban Coalition, NAHB, and the Mortgage Bankers Association.
